title: Cranberry and orange scones
subtitle: Dried cranberry and orange zest scones, lightly sweetened
excerpt: A standard British scone dough with dried cranberries and fresh orange zest worked through it. Served warm with butter, or with clotted cream and jam in the usual way. The orange lifts the cranberry from sweet-sharp to something more fragrant.
sourceNotes: Cranberry and orange is a well-established flavour pairing in British baking, most common in muffins and festive loaves. The application here follows the standard British scone method, which dates to the eighteenth century as a quick bread leavened with baking powder. The addition of dried fruit to scones is a long-standing British tradition (the sultana scone being the most common). The cranberry-orange variant is a modern home-baking adaptation.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked scones once cool. Reheat from frozen in a 180 degrees C oven for 10 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Scones are best eaten the day they are baked. The dry ingredients can be combined the day before; add the wet ingredients just before baking.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; scones bake quickly at high heat for a good rise and colour

glossaryTerms.definition: Cold butter rubbed into flour creates small pockets of fat that steam during baking, producing the characteristic layered lift of a well-made scone. If the butter warms and merges fully into the flour, the result is a denser, more bread-like crumb. Work quickly with cold hands, or use a box grater to grate frozen butter directly into the flour.

content.text: The principle behind any good scone is
and wet ingredients added just until the dough comes together. Do not knead. Do not overwork. Pat the dough out to an even thickness and cut straight down without twisting the cutter.

Heat the oven to 220 degrees C fan. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Combine 250 g flour, 2 tsp baking powder, and 0.25 tsp salt in a bowl. Rub in 50 g cold butter until the mixture resembles rough breadcrumbs. Stir in 50 g caster sugar, 75 g dried cranberries, and the zest of 1 orange. Beat the egg with the buttermilk and add most of it to the bowl. Mix with a table knife until the dough just comes together; add the remaining liquid if it feels dry. The dough should be soft but not sticky.

Turn out onto a lightly floured surface and pat to 3 cm thick. Do not roll. Stamp out rounds with a 6 cm cutter, pressing straight down without twisting. Re-pat the offcuts once only. Place on the lined tray. Brush the tops with the reserved beaten egg. Bake for 12 to 14 minutes until risen and golden. Cool on a wire rack for at least 10 minutes before splitting.

items.solution: The dough was overworked, the baking powder is stale, or the oven was not fully preheated. Baking powder loses potency after 6 months. Cut straight down with the cutter: twisting seals the edge and prevents rising.

items.problem: Cranberries sink to the bottom during baking
items.solution: The dough is too wet. Toss the cranberries in a little flour before adding them to the mix; the coating helps them stay distributed through the dough.
